verificationNote: Purdue model is publicly documented in ISA-99 and ISA-95 standards. NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3 Chapter 4 provides extensive architecture guidance.
description: The Purdue Model defines a hierarchical reference architecture for Industrial Control Systems, dividing OT and IT functions into discrete layers with defined data flows. Originally defined in the 1990s, it remains the dominant reference model for OT security zone design and is the basis for IEC 62443 zone/conduit architecture. Modern adaptations address cloud connectivity, IIoT, and zero-trust.
modernConsiderations: Modern OT environments increasingly use cloud connectivity, edge computing, and IIoT devices that span traditional Purdue boundaries. The IDMZ (Level 3.5) is an extension added specifically to address IT/OT convergence. Zero-trust architecture principles are increasingly applied within OT but the zone-based Purdue model remains the dominant framework.

levels.name: Field Level
levels.description: The physical process itself — the sensors that measure it and the actuators that control it. This level is the interface between the cyber and physical domains.

securityCharacteristics.attackSurface: Physical tampering, sensor spoofing, actuator manipulation
securityCharacteristics.vulnerabilities: No authentication in analog signals; HART and Fieldbus have limited security; physical access often insufficient controlled
primaryControls: Physical access control
Tamper detection
Redundant measurement channels
Independent high-high/low-low safety interlocks
securityCharacteristics.targetSL: Physical security controls; SL requirements applied at Level 1 boundary

levels.nacsa: Physical assets at Level 0 are the ultimate protection target for NCII-designated entities — the systems whose compromise has direct public safety or economic consequences.

levels.name: Basic Control Level
levels.description: The controllers that execute control logic and interface directly with Level 0 field devices. This is where process control algorithms execute in real time.

securityCharacteristics.attackSurface: PLC program modification, setpoint manipulation, firmware replacement, DoS, protocol exploitation
securityCharacteristics.vulnerabilities: Limited native authentication; legacy devices have no network-layer authentication; real-time OS constraints limit security capabilities
primaryControls: Network segmentation from Level 2
Change management for all program changes
Firmware integrity verification
Physical access control to controller cabinets
securityCharacteristics.targetSL: SL 2 minimum for standard PLCs; SL 3 for critical process PLCs; SL 4 for SIS

levels.nacsa: Level 1 controllers are the primary target for process manipulation attacks. NCII-designated operators must include all Level 1 controllers in their NCII asset register (NACSA Act 854 s17, s18).

levels.name: Supervisory Control Level
levels.description: Human-machine interfaces and supervisory control systems that provide operator visibility and control over the industrial process. Operators interact with the process through Level 2 systems.

securityCharacteristics.attackSurface: Windows OS vulnerabilities, HMI application vulnerabilities, engineering tool exploitation, operator workstation malware
securityCharacteristics.vulnerabilities: Windows-based systems often unpatched; HMI software may have known vulnerabilities; operator workstations may have internet access; engineering workstations have direct PLC programming access
primaryControls: Application whitelisting
Patch management (OT-vendor-approved patches)
Network segmentation from Level 3
Jump server for engineering workstation access
No internet access on OT workstations
securityCharacteristics.targetSL: SL 2 standard; SL 3 for critical infrastructure SCADA

levels.nacsa: Level 2 is the primary zone for NACSA s23 audit evidence collection — SCADA configurations, operator logs, and alarm records are key audit artefacts.

levels.name: Site Operations and Manufacturing Operations
levels.description: Site-level systems that aggregate data from multiple Level 2 systems, provide site-wide visibility, manage production scheduling, and interface with business systems. The historian typically lives here.

securityCharacteristics.attackSurface: Historian exploitation, SQL database vulnerabilities, MES web application vulnerabilities, lateral movement from IT
securityCharacteristics.vulnerabilities: Historian servers often bridging IT and OT; MES may have web interfaces accessible from corporate network; SQL databases may be inadequately secured
primaryControls: Separate network segment from Level 2 and Level 4
Historian in IDMZ (preferred) or with strict firewall rules
Database hardening
No direct connectivity to internet or corporate email

levels.nacsa: Historian data is key evidence for NACSA Act 854 s21 risk assessments and s23 audits — process data demonstrates system operation and supports anomaly detection.

levels.name: Industrial DMZ (IDMZ)
levels.description: A purpose-built buffer zone between OT (Levels 0-3) and IT (Levels 4-5). The IDMZ contains services that need to exchange data between OT and IT without providing direct network connectivity between the two environments. All data exchange between OT and IT must pass through the IDMZ.

typicalComponents.purpose: Hardware-enforced one-way data flow from OT to IT — physically impossible for data to flow in reverse
Single controlled access point for IT-to-OT remote administration
Replicates historian data from OT historian to IT-accessible copy without direct connection
Receives patches from IT/internet, scans and validates, distributes to OT without direct internet-to-OT connectivity
Passive OT network monitoring — collects and analyses OT traffic without sending packets into control networks
Controlled and monitored remote access for vendor maintenance with session recording

securityCharacteristics.attackSurface: IDMZ systems themselves, data flow from IT to OT through IDMZ services
securityCharacteristics.vulnerabilities: IDMZ misconfiguration creating direct IT-OT paths; overly permissive firewall rules; IDMZ systems left unmanaged
primaryControls: Deny-by-default firewall rules at both IDMZ boundaries
Regular review of all permitted traffic flows
IDMZ systems included in patch management scope
Session recording for all jump server access
securityCharacteristics.targetSL: SL 2-3 — IDMZ is a critical security zone, not just a transit zone

levels.nacsa: The IDMZ is the primary enforcement point for NACSA Act 854 s18 data security measures for NCII entities with OT environments. All cross-boundary data flows should be documented as part of s21 risk assessment evidence.

levels.name: Business Logistics / Site Business Planning
levels.description: Business information systems — ERP, supply chain management, business analytics, finance. This is where traditional IT security applies. Level 4 is the first IT level and should never have direct connectivity to OT levels 0-3.

securityCharacteristics.attackSurface: Standard IT attack surface — phishing, web exploitation, credential theft
securityCharacteristics.vulnerabilities: Lateral movement from IT to OT is the dominant attack vector in major OT incidents
primaryControls: IDMZ enforces separation from Level 3
No direct connectivity to OT levels
Corporate IT security standard controls
securityCharacteristics.targetSL: Standard IT security — not IEC 62443 scope; addressed by ISO 27001 / NIST CSF

levels.nacsa: Level 4 systems at NCII-designated entities are within scope of NACSA Act 854 obligations to the extent they are interconnected with designated NCII systems.

levels.name: Enterprise Network and Internet
levels.description: The enterprise WAN, internet connectivity, cloud services, and external partner connections. Should have no direct connectivity to OT levels.

securityCharacteristics.attackSurface: Internet-facing attack surface — all internet threats apply
primaryControls: No direct connectivity to OT environment
All OT remote access through IDMZ jump server
Cloud OT connectivity through industrial IoT platforms with OT security controls
